posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Of Curnonsky’s famous list of France’s five greatest white wines, I have the least experience with Château Grillet. In fact, I’ve never even visited (yet). I’ve also had mixed experiences, with some rather ephemeral wines from one era, and some rather oaky ones from another. But tonight all that changed. I opened a bottle without a label, and the cork is only marked “Château Grillet”. But the bottle is 75cl, not 73cl, and the capsule was lead, not tin; so I’m inclined to situate this in the late 1970s, maybe '78 or '79 given the quality. And the quality is quite extraordinary. Firstly the wine appears, visually, to be about five years old. There’s zero oxidation. The exotic qualities of Viognier are fully kept in check: aromatically, think white peach, waxy lemon rind, hints of rose petals, wet stones, pine nuts. The palate is suave, glossy and textural but beautifully fresh, to the extent that you could swear it grew on limestone; it’s weightless, and unerringly precise. Received opinions are followed too slavishly in wine, but Curnonsky’s taxonomy is starting to make more sense to me.

So I was wondering, what have people’s experiences been with old Grillet? How do the different eras of the estate’s history break down? Does anyone drink this stuff?

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
TN: A Château-Grillet vertical, a 1994 Northern Rhône Syrah horizontal + stuff WINE TALK
Originally this was a dual tasting of five different vintages of Château-Grillet and three bottles of 1994 Northern Rhône Syrah. However, as always, people brought some extra bottles with them and the original lineup got extended quite a bit during the process. I brought that Hudelot-Noëllat Bourgogne and served it blind - while a very lovely wine indeed, it didn’t really scream “Burgundy” or “Pinot Noir” from the get go and many people were quite confused for some time before it was finally cor…

Here we go.

Btw, that 1979 we had was bottled in a 0,70-liter bottle. I’d imagine they changed to 0,75 later on.

And I do agree that these older Grillets can be remarkably youthful for their age! I was really surprised how youthful most these seemes, at least for their age.

link_counts.title: TN: A Château-Grillet vertical, a 1994 Northern Rhône Syrah horizontal + stuff

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
We may be narrowing it down, then, because they wouldn’t have used a lead capsule after 1982…

I’ve asked the estate if they have any insights.

70 cl is odd, though, 73 would have been more common

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

70 cl is odd, though, 73 would have been more common

Yeah, go figure. The bottle looked slightly different from the younger bottles we had, but mainly due to the different colored glass. It was a tiny bit shorter and it might’ve been slightly slimmer, but it’s perfectly possible that it has been the same bottle size during that time, but they’ve just labeled the wine with 70 cl:

posts.cooked: I was just messaged with a photo of a 1983 Grillet bottle that had “0.7l” stamped on the bottle, so it seems there have been differently sized bottles circulating around, even in a single vintage!

posts.name: Stephen Williams
posts.cooked:
I drank this last month at my house in France. 75cl. I recall the bottle size and shape/ weight changing in the mid 80s. I used to pick up 12 bottles every year when I traveled with my parents from Northern Spain to the UK. At first the owner (Neyret-Gachet) was quite offish for reasons I’ll keep to myself but once we resolved his concern he welcomed us into the cellar and we enjoyed many hours of tasting. The old bottles were very special but you could sense that the contemporary ones (from the late 70s and early 80s) lacked the complexity and concentration of earlier years.

The 1990 we drank last month was as fresh as a daisy. It would stand head and shoulders with many 1990 White Burgundies. Old bottles are frequently available at auction in France. I’d have no hesitation buying bottles back into the 70s.

IMG_60063024×4032 3.99 MB

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Interesting! Did you note if the capsule was lead or tin?

Mine was lead, which is seemingly at odds with the 75 cl bottle…

posts.name: Andy K0b3rl
posts.cooked: What are the vintages to look out for? You mentioned the late 70s and early 80s were lacking complexity when you tasted them (presumably young)…

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
In 1990, when I was living in Geneva, there was a Christie’s auction. I had never attended an auction, and screwed up the only lot I had a winning bid on, ending up with two cases of Canon 1982 rather than the Latour ‘85 I thought I was buying. I have never regretted it.

At the pre tasting though, there were some interesting older wines, including an 1899 Grillet. The color was medium brown, and the nose a little Maderized but you could still smell and taste the lychees and apricots. The estimate was low, and even though this time I was bidding on the right wine, it went way beyond what I was prepared to pay.

posts.name: Stephen Williams
posts.cooked:
I can’t be certain about the capsule but I’m pretty sure it was lead. I’ll check my other bottles when I’m back in France next month.

As for vintages, some of my favourites are 1970 (Renaissance bottling), 1971, 1978, 1979, 1985, 1988, 1990. I’m not a fan of their 1990s wine in general although I’m sure I’ll be proved wrong at some stage !

posts.name: Noah C @ P U R 5 0
posts.cooked:
Had a bottle of 1982 Grillet today. Incredible wine. I wrote my notes before reading William Kelley’s notes, but as you can see below, I also had descriptors of wax, lemons, white peach, and limestone, as well as commenting on the wines amazing freshness. For what it’s worth, it was 70cl bottle with a tin capsule.

My TN from CellarTracker:
Cork covered with dark mold and sunk a good 1/8" below the rim of the bottle. Nonetheless, the wine inside was perfect. Incredibly youthful appearing- luminous pale yellow, super clear.

The nose is unlike anything I’ve ever smelled. There is very little fruit here. It’s definitely waxy, just like a beeswax candle. Underneath are layers of slightly bitter smells like citrus pith and a bit of flint. Surprisingly, despite its age, there are beautiful white florals. There’s also pleasant creamy and fermented creme fraiche aromas. Only then, underneath it all, are there aromas of preserved lemons, oranges, peach, and fresh apricots.

Unbelievable that this is Viognier when it is tasted. It is light in body, none of the thick viscosity or oily texture of Condrieu. Acidity is medium, which is more than a typical Condrieu. The flavors are waxy, with a hint of floral perfume which is in line with Viognier, and so many other subtle flavors swimming beneath; apricot, sour creme, lemon desserts, white peaches, a touch of honey, citrus zest, lots of limestone-like minerals. Not a hint of those off-putting “old wine” flavors that are common in aged bottles. Somehow, the age has made for an incredible complex wine without sacrificing any freshness. The finish lingers for a long time.

This is simply an amazing bottle of wine. A 43 year old Viognier that is super fresh and delicious. I can see it getting swallowed up in a lineup with lots of other wines, though; the complexities are subtle- I’m so glad I drank this at dinner without other wines to distract. Two big downsides; 1- how can I ever source more old Grillet, and 2- how can I ensure it’s in nearly as good shape as this? I’ll consider myself lucky to have had something like this even once.

A perfect pairing to lobster risotto and a side of braised leeks.

posts.name: Noah C @ P U R 5 0
posts.cooked:
Indeed! Do you have a sense of the reliability of these old Grillets? Did I hit the aged Viognier lottery with this, or are a decent number of these drinking well?

Also, I just took a flyer on an 80s Vernay Coteaux de Vernon. We’ll see how that goes- I’ll report back here!

posts.name: Stephen Williams
posts.cooked:
I’ve had a great deal of success with old Grillets, all the way back to 1971. I can’t recall a single bottle being over the hill or out of condition. Experiences with Condrieu have been a bit more mixed. For example, a 1990 Chateau de Rozay (the Domaine had since slipped into the sands of time) was spectacular last year. A great colour and remarkable acidity, something often missing with present day Condrieus.

A 2004 Vernon, in 2022 was OTT

A 2010 Grillet this past weekend was still on the young side and needed 3 hours in the decanter to blossom.
